nativeRange.habitat: Small tributaries, creeks, areas of flooded forest, and sandbars in the Rio Meta basin; clear to slightly turbid, well-oxygenated, slow-moving lowland streams

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate to protect sensitive barbels
leaf litter and driftwood for cover and biofilm
moderate planting (Java fern, Anubias, Amazon swords)
shaded areas and caves for retreat

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of sinking foods: high-quality sinking pellets and wafers as the staple diet. Supplement regularly with live or frozen Daphnia, Artemia (brine shrimp), bloodworm, and tubifex. Also forages on biofilm, organic detritus, and microorganisms naturally present in a mature tank. Feed once or twice daily, ensuring food reaches the substrate; target-feed if kept with faster mid-water species.

goodWith: small tetras (neon tetra, ember tetra, cardinal tetra)
dwarf cichlids (Apistogramma spp.) in spacious tanks
dwarf shrimp (with caution — fry may be taken)
avoidWith: fish that compete aggressively for bottom-dwelling food
very high-temperature tankmates (above 27°C conflicts with this species’ cool-water preference)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo for Corydoras melanotaenia; confirm URL resolves before selecting.

summary: The Gold Laser Cory is a slender, peaceful armored catfish from Colombia’s Rio Meta basin, prized for the vivid iridescent gold-green stripe running the length of its body. Reaching about 5–6 cm, it thrives in groups of six or more and favors slightly cooler, soft, acidic water. An excellent community fish for nano and planted tanks.
careNotes: The Gold Laser Cory (Corydoras melanotaenia) is named for the brilliant iridescent gold-green lateral stripe that runs from the snout to the caudal peduncle, especially vivid under aquarium lighting. It is a slender, elongate species within the armored catfish subfamily Corydoradinae and reaches approximately 5–6 cm. Keep in groups of at least six; smaller groups cause stress, reduce activity, and increase susceptibility to disease. A 60-liter aquarium with a fine sand substrate is the recommended minimum, as this species is particularly sensitive to coarse or sharp substrate that damages the soft barbels used for foraging — eroded barbels are an early indicator of poor substrate conditions. The Gold Laser Cory prefers slightly cooler temperatures (20–25°C) than many popular Corydoras species; this is an important consideration when selecting tankmates. Maintain soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.2, gH 2–15) with good oxygenation and moderate flow. Weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are recommended. Like all Corydoras, this species is copper-sensitive; avoid copper-based medications. Handle with care — the pectoral and dorsal fin spines can deliver a mild venom when the fish is stressed. Taxonomic note: the 2024 phylogenomic revision by Dias et al. (Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society) placed this species in the resurrected genus Osteogaster as Osteogaster melanotaenia; the user brief notes a pending Hoplisoma placement, but published sources confirm Osteogaster. The species is widely sold in the hobby under the trade name ‘Gold Laser Cory’ or ‘Green Gold Cory’.
breedingNotes: Breeding follows the classic Corydoradinae ‘T-position’ method: the male fertilises eggs held between the female’s pelvic fins, which she then deposits on smooth surfaces including glass, broad leaves, or smooth decor. Condition adults on a varied diet of live and frozen foods for 2–3 weeks before attempting to breed. A dedicated breeding tank (approximately 40–60 L) with slightly acidic, soft water, moderate flow, and a fine sand or bare-bottom substrate is recommended. Lower the temperature slightly (to the 20–22°C range) and perform a larger water change to simulate seasonal rainfall cues. A ratio of two males per female, or two females per male, is commonly reported as effective. Eggs are adhesive; they can be left with adults in heavily planted tanks or transferred to a separate rearing vessel to avoid predation. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–5 days at 22–24°C; first foods for fry include infusoria, microworm, and baby brine shrimp nauplii. Breeding is rated moderate due to the need for specific conditioning and water parameter management.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras melanotaenia
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (6 cm SL), temperature 20–23°C, pH 6.0–7.2, hardness 2–15°H, minimum tank dimensions (45×30×30 cm / ~42.5 L), native range (Rio Meta basin, Colombia), diet, compatibility (groups of 6+), and breeding notes. Temperature upper bound extended to 25°C based on FishBase (23–25°C) and Tankably (22–26°C) to reflect practical hobby range.

additional.name: FishBase — Corydoras melanotaenia
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), adult size (5.8 cm SL male/unsexed), temperature 23–25°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 2–25 dH, native range (Meta River basin, Colombia), demersal/facultative air-breathing habit.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Corydoras Catfish Care
additional.notes: General Corydoras care guide confirming omnivore diet, groups of 6+, fine sand substrate requirement, venomous/sharp fin spines noted ('sharp spines that can sometimes produce a mild venom when stressed'), and copper sensitivity. No species-specific page for C. melanotaenia available.

additional.name: Tankably — Osteogaster melanotaenia
additional.notes: Post-revision profile using accepted name Osteogaster melanotaenia. Provided adult size 5–7 cm, temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 36–215 ppm, minimum tank 70 L, lifespan 7 years, breeding difficulty moderate (T-position method), diet details (sinking pellets, biofilm, microorganisms, small invertebrates, detritus).

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser GmbH — Corydoras have been revised: new genus names
additional.notes: Confirmed 2024 Dias et al. phylogenomic revision (Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society). Corydoras melanotaenia placed in resurrected genus Osteogaster as O. melanotaenia, alongside O. aeneus, O. eques (type species), O. hephaestus, O. maclurei, O. rabauti, and O. zygatus.

fish.breedingNotes: Classic Corydoradinae T-position spawning; condition adults on live/frozen foods, lower temperature and perform large water change to trigger spawning. Eggs hatch in 3–5 days; fry accept microworm and baby brine shrimp nauplii. Separate eggs or fry to avoid predation.
